title: Agent Engineering: System Designs
author: Minhajul Hoque
text:
Agent Engineering: System Designs
When to use a single agent vs multi-agent systems, using task properties and measured trade-offs, not vibes.
If you’ve built an agentic system and kept adding subagents hoping it would “just work,” this post is for you.
tl;dr
- Pick a Single-Agent System (SAS) when the task is sequential, stateful, or tool-heavy (>16 tools). One coherent state beats coordination. MAS often fragments context and burns budget on synchronization.
- Pick a Multi-Agent System (MAS) when the work is truly parallelizable and intermediate outputs are easy to verify (schemas, invariants, unit tests, evidence packets).
- Avoid “independent + merge” (or “vote/merge”) unless you have a hard quality gate: structured contracts + a verifier that can reject outputs, not just average them. Otherwise you amplify errors.
- Treat coordination like a budgeted resource:
System performance ≈ baseline capability + decomposition benefit − coordination tax
Decomposition can help, but coordination costs (extra turns, messages, state sync, redundancy, lossy summaries) compound fast. - Most real systems are hybrid: keep a SAS driver for the main sequential loop, and use bounded MAS helpers for context-light, checkable subtasks (parallel retrieval, alternative drafts, targeted verification).
- Under matched budgets, turn count grows superlinearly as you add agents , so “more agents” is rarely free for real-world use cases.
1. What is an agent (and what is “agent engineering”?)
An agent is an LLM wrapped in a closed-loop control system that can take actions, observe outcomes, and iterate until it reaches a goal.
If the LLM does not adapt to external feedback, it is not an agent. It is a workflow that just runs steps.
Agency needs:
- World interaction: it can change the environment via tools, then adapt to what comes back
- Explicit control: objectives plus termination rules, done, failed, budget, safety
1.1 What is “agent engineering”?
Agent engineering is system design that turns a capable but uneven model into a reliable task finisher under real constraints: messy inputs, long horizons, imperfect tools, and hard budgets.
The primitives you actually engineer
- State: memory, scratchpad, task context, and what must persist
- Tools plus contracts: schemas, permissions, retries, timeouts
- Policy and planning: sequencing, delegation, escalation
- Decomposition: split for parallelism or specialization
- Context strategy: include, retrieve, summarize, or omit
- Verification: validators, invariants, consistency checks, verifiers
- Recovery: fallbacks, re-tries with constraints, alternate tools
- Budgets plus stops: caps on turns, tokens, time, and fail-safe behavior
1.2 Why agent engineering exists
Agentic systems are not better by default. They exist because real tasks are messy, multi-step, and stateful, while LLM capability is spiky.
LLMs don’t fail gracefully. They spike in some regions of capability and faceplant in others , often within the same task. Karpathy describes this as jagged performance: models can look like “a genius polymath” and then behave like “a confused grade schooler” minutes later.
That creates a predictable product reality:
- One-shot prompting works on clean slices.
- End-to-end reliability collapses when tasks require long context, tool chains, edge cases, verification, or recovery.
So agent engineering is scaffolding. You are not trying to make the model smarter. You are building bridges between its high-skill regions so it can move through a task without falling off a cliff.
Better models reduce how much scaffolding you need per step, but not the need for scaffolding across steps. Single agents still overload (context rot/noise, instruction following issues, attention drift, sequential latency). Agentic systems still win on throughput (parallelism, specialization, separation of concerns). Long-horizon work also compounds small error rates, so tiny per-step gains matter at scale. You can find more in-depth discussion about this topic in my previous blog post.
2. Single agent vs Multi-agent
The choice is not “more agents = better.” It’s a trade-off (like any good ol’ engineering).
A good formula to remember would be:
System performance ≈ baseline capability + decomposition benefit − coordination tax
- Baseline capability: what a single-agent system (SAS) can do end-to-end with a coherent state.
- Decomposition benefit: gains from splitting into cleaner subproblems (parallel evidence, specialization, independent cross-checking, reduced context noise)
- Coordination tax: overhead to make many agents behave like one system (extra turns, message passing, state sync, duplicated tool calls, lossy summaries)
Measured overhead show up fast:
- Anthropic reported some multi-agent setups consumed ~15× more tokens than a single agent for certain tasks.
- In “Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems,” matched token budgets still led to far more turns for MAS topologies. Hybrid averaged 44.3 turns vs 7.2 for SAS. Centralized and Decentralized were about 27.7 and 26.1.
- Turn count grows superlinearly with agent count. Under fixed budgets, beyond roughly 3 to 4 agents, per-agent reasoning thins and communication overhead dominates.
2.1 When multi-agent helps (and when it hurts)
“Task complexity” is a bad predictor. What matters is task shape.
- Multi-agent tends to help when the work is decomposable: you can split it into mostly independent streams that recombine cleanly, with minimal back and forth.
- Single-agent tends to win when the work has high sequential dependence: later steps must respect a precise evolving state produced by earlier steps.
A quick test: picture the task’s context as a single sheet of paper.
- If you can cut it into clean, independent pieces and each agent can finish its piece without reaching into anyone else’s, the task is truly parallel and a multi-agent system can exploit that.
- If agents keep needing the same shared area to do good work, the task is sequential and stateful. Multi-agent can still work, but now you are building machinery for dependencies: passing the right state at the right time, resolving conflicts, and keeping everyone consistent. In those cases, it is usually simpler and more reliable to let one single agent carry the evolving state end to end.
In practice, decomposition benefit usually comes from one of four pressures:
- Context limits: the full problem does not fit cleanly in one window.
- Context noise: relevant constraints get buried and dropped.
- Instruction overload: too many rules in one place makes behavior brittle.
- Edge-of-capability stacks: the model can solve the parts but not the full chain, so you move structure into the system.
2.2 Concrete examples
“Find all board members for 200 companies” is naturally parallel. Each company lookup is independent, and the intermediate outputs are easy to verify against sources. Anthropic uses this exact shape as a motivating example for multi-agent research.
“Make a sequence of dependent edits to a codebase” is usually not. Each action changes the state, and later steps depend on exact earlier decisions, so parallel workers constantly need updates and conflict resolution. Cognition’s core warning is that agent reliability is mostly context engineering — and naive decomposition breaks shared context.
3. Agentic System Designs
At a high level, you have two families of systems: a Single-Agent System (SAS) that keeps one coherent brain and one evolving state, and a Multi-Agent System (MAS) that splits work across multiple brains and then recombines results. MAS is not one thing. The architecture you pick determines where you pay overhead, where you gain parallelism, and how errors propagate.
3.1 Single-Agent System (SAS)
SAS is the default when the task is stateful or sequential, where later steps depend heavily on earlier decisions and tool outputs.
Best for:
- Unified context: one continuous state, no cross-agent synchronization.
- Simplest control loop: fewer moving parts, fewer failure surfaces.
- Best for sequential tasks: planning, coding, long tool chains, environment interaction.
Trade-off:
- Context limits: long-running work can hit window constraints.
- Slow for breadth: research-style tasks that could be parallelized stay sequential.
- Instruction overload: one prompt accumulating many roles becomes brittle.
3.2 Independent Multi-Agent System (MAS)
Independent MAS runs multiple agents in parallel and merges outputs at the end. Use it only when the task cleanly decomposes and the merge is mechanically safe.
Best for:
- Parallel information gathering where each slice is self-contained (e.g., summarize N docs independently).
- Generating diverse candidates when you have a strong downstream selector or verifier.
Trade-off:
- Error amplification + redundancy: agents repeat work, drift, or hallucinate differently, and the merge step has no “quality gate.” Without verification, bad outputs compound instead of canceling out.
3.3 Centralized Multi-Agent System (MAS)
Centralized MAS adds an orchestrator that assigns subtasks and integrates results. This is the most common “serious” MAS pattern because it gives you parallelism while keeping a single point of synthesis.
Best for:
- Parallel work where intermediate outputs must be checked and reconciled.
- Systems that need consistent formatting, tool discipline, or schema adherence.
Mechanism:
- The orchestrator acts as a quality gate: it validates, cross-checks, and decides what enters shared state. This reduces drift and contains errors compared to naive merging.
Trade-offs:
- Extra coordination overhead: more turns and message traffic
- Bottleneck risk: synthesis can limit throughput if the orchestrator is overloaded
- Coordinator spends budget reading and reconciling, not advancing the environment.
3.4 Decentralized Multi-Agent System (MAS)
Decentralized MAS replaces a single judge with peer interaction: agents challenge and refine each other’s proposals. It can be valuable when the problem is open-ended and benefits from adversarial review.
Best for:
- Exploratory reasoning where multiple perspectives uncover blind spots.
- Situations where “argue and converge” improves the final plan or interpretation.
Trade-off:
- High message overhead: debate creates turns, coordination, and state sync costs. Gains are strongly task-dependent, and the system can burn budget arguing without moving the environment forward.
3.5 Hybrid Multi-Agent System (MAS)
Hybrid MAS mixes patterns: a central synthesizer with selective peer review, or parallel workers with occasional debate, or staged pipelines (research → draft → verify).
Best for:
- Real products: you can dial how much parallelism and verification you want.
- Domains where pure MAS often hurts, but targeted decomposition still helps.
Why it works:
- It’s a practical compromise: enough structure to prevent chaos, enough parallelism to reduce latency, and enough verification to prevent silent failures.
4. The mechanics that actually decide outcomes
SAS vs MAS is less about “how hard is the task” and more about three mechanics: communication vs coordination, coordination tax, and context management (or the Triple Cs…).
4.1 Communication vs Coordination
Communication is moving information between agents. Coordination is aligning actions when steps depend on each other. You can communicate constantly and still coordinate badly.
Company analogy: two engineers can talk and ship. Ten engineers do not ship five times faster. They need interfaces, ownership, versioning, reviews, and conflict resolution. Output is not headcount. It is headcount minus the overhead of making that headcount move as one.
Agents work the same way:
- Communication is “sharing notes”
- Coordination is ensuring the right notes arrive at the right time, in the right format, and actually change the next action.
4.2 Coordination Tax
Mental model: Agents don’t scale. Coordination scales.
A simple way to reason about it:
System performance ≈ baseline capability + decomposition benefit − coordination tax
Baseline capability is what one agent can do while holding a coherent state.
Decomposition benefit comes from real separability: parallel evidence gathering, specialization, cross-checks, and reduced context noise.
Coordination tax shows up as:
- More turns spent aligning before acting
- More handoffs and message passing
- State synchronization costs (who knows what, when)
- Duplicated reasoning and tool calls
- Lossy summaries that drop details that matter later
Without matched budgets, MAS can feel faster while quietly spending most compute on overhead.
4.3 Context Management
In agent systems, context is the finite budget (for now…). Every token spent replaying state is a token not spent on reasoning, tool use, or verification. The core design question:
How does the system carry state forward without drowning itself in it?
4.3.1 SAS context: coherence by default
A single agent has one advantage that matters: one evolving state. The same model sees the same constraints and tool outputs turn after turn. This is why SAS is strong for sequential, stateful work like coding, long tool chains, and environment interactions.
The failure mode is also simple: the context window fills. You either keep everything and dilute attention, or trim and drop context.
SAS works best when the active state stays small, explicit, and stable.
4.3.2 MAS context: parallelization by default
Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) don’t have “one brain with one memory.” They have multiple partial views that must be aligned. That makes context management the central design problem, not an implementation detail.
In MAS you typically end up in one of two extremes:
4.3.2.1 Context broadcasting (full trace)
Workers send back a full trace (or near-full trace) to the orchestrator or other agents.
Pros
- High fidelity: fewer missing constraints, fewer “why did you assume that?” errors
- Better for brittle tasks where one omitted detail breaks downstream steps
Cons
- Token explosion: you pay to copy context N times
- Less room for new work: reasoning and tool outputs get squeezed
Broadcasting preserves coherence (but can add a lot of noise), but it can bankrupt your budget.
4.3.2.2 Context summarization (compressed)
This option is much more common in industry. Workers send back a synthesized response rather than the full trace.
Pros
- Reduce total context usage for main agent
- Helps when you’re doing breadth-first work (many sources, many parallel threads)
Cons
- Lossy by default: missing one constraint can cause wrong assumptions
- Tool chaining degrades: downstream tool calls often depend on details summaries omit
- Errors become quiet: a clean summary can hide shaky evidence and uncertainty
Summarization buys cost and speed by potentially sacrificing accuracy and recoverability.
5. Evidence from Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems
This post is motivated by “Towards a Science of Scaling Agent Systems”, which treats agent architecture as an engineering variable and runs controlled comparisons under matched token budgets.
It evaluates five architectures (SAS plus Independent, Centralized, Decentralized, Hybrid MAS) across three LLM families, totaling 180 configurations. Beyond task success, it measures coordination behaviors (efficiency, overhead, redundancy, error amplification) and fits a model linking performance to measurable task properties.
Why fixed budgets matter:
- Multi-agent can look great when you let it debate forever.
- Production has ceilings: latency, tokens, dollars.
- The question is: does coordination buy progress per unit compute, or burn the budget on overhead?
5.1 Caveats
The paper standardizes implementations to isolate architecture effects. It is not a contest of custom prompts, fancy role design, or domain-specific tooling. Read “which architecture wins” as evidence under standardized, matched-budget conditions, not a universal ranking. Real systems often tune prompts, assign specialized roles, add task-specific tools, or relax budgets, all of which can shift results.
The more durable value is the intuition: parallelizable vs. sequential tasks, coordination overhead, and why context management becomes the real cost center as you add agents.
5.2 Benchmarks
To understand the results, you only need each benchmark’s shape , what kind of state it creates and how decomposable it is.
- Finance-Agent: multi-step financial reasoning and analysis in a structured domain.
- BrowseComp-Plus: dynamic web navigation, information extraction, and cross-page synthesis.
- Workbench: workflow execution with tool use and objective pass/fail criteria.
- PlanCraft: planning under constraints where actions change state and later steps depend on earlier inventory/state transitions.
6. Key findings
6.1 Tool-coordination trade-off (tool-heavy tasks punish MAS)
Under fixed token budgets, tool-heavy tasks magnify coordination tax. The paper highlights this explicitly for environments with T = 16 tools (e.g., Workbench): multi-agent systems pay a disproportionate efficiency penalty as tool count rises.
Tools create state (observations, intermediate results, constraints). In MAS that state must be synchronized. Turn count grows superlinearly with agent count (power-law exponent ≈ 1.724), so adding agents increases coordination turns faster than it increases useful work.
Because budgets are matched, extra turns mean thinner reasoning per decision and weaker tool orchestration: less capacity to interpret outputs, plan the next call, and maintain invariants across a tool chain.
Context strategy just changes how you fail:
- Broadcast full traces: preserves detail, but explodes tokens and starves execution.
- Summarize aggressively: reduces tokens, but loses dependencies — exactly what tool chaining needs.
The paper shows the canonical failure pattern: MAS “decomposes” a sequential task into redundant subtasks (e.g., “research recipe,” “check inventory”), spending budget on coordination instead of acting. In tool-heavy settings, the analogue is multiple agents re-calling overlapping tools or re-checking the same state.
Nuance: these results are under standardized implementations. More advanced designs , e.g., routing to specialized subagents with restricted toolsets , could reduce overhead in some domains, but that is not what’s measured here.
6.2 Capability saturation (“baseline paradox” ~45%)
Coordination is not free upside. Once the single-agent baseline exceeds ~45%, the paper finds that adding coordination yields diminishing or negative returns.
Why this happens is simple:
- Headroom shrinks: the maximum possible gain is bounded by (1 − p).
- Residual errors get correlated: as the model improves, remaining failures are often the same hard edge cases across agents.
- Coordination cost is mostly fixed: extra turns, messages, and integration don’t disappear just because the task is easier.
Expect MAS accuracy gains mainly when you have real headroom and errors are plausibly diverse/uncorrelated. Use MAS for other reasons (latency, context limits, composability), but don’t assume it will lift success rate.
6.3 Architecture-dependent error dynamics
“Multi-agent” is not a single technique. The architecture determines whether mistakes are absorbed or amplified.
The paper’s strongest warning is about Independent “parallel + merge” systems: without any verification chokepoint, errors propagate unchecked and compound. Quantitatively, Independent setups amplify errors 17.2× relative to a single-agent baseline , while Centralized coordination contains amplification to 4.4× by enforcing supervised aggregation.
Architectures that build in explicit verification reduce errors because they force disagreement to be resolved before outputs enter shared state. In Decentralized systems, debate acts as a challenge–response check; the paper reports 22.7% average error reduction, peaking at 31.4% on Finance Agent where structured numerical outputs make verification easier.
6.3.1 What does verification mean?
Verification is a reject-capable gate before outputs become shared state. Not averaging. Not “pick the nicest phrasing.” A mechanism that can say “fails the contract” and force a redo or demand evidence.
In practice this is often an orchestrator that aggregates subagent outputs and checks them against instructions and requirements.
Rule of thumb: if correctness matters, don’t run Independent + merge without real verification. Use contracts plus a verifier that can reject, not just blend.
6.4 Context Anxiousness
Models often exhibit budget pressure behavior: as they believe they’re nearing the context limit, they often stop exploring, shorten reasoning, and skip extra tool calls. People call this “context anxiety,” and it matches what many builders see in long-horizon tasks. You can read how Cognition ran into the similar issues when migrating to Sonnet 4.5.
I’ve seen it firsthand in agentic work and in day-to-day use in Cursor: mid-refactor, the model starts “wrapping up,” leaves a half-finished to-do list, and exits like it heard the closing music.
The paper includes a single-agent system FinanceAgent run that stays at surface-level search; context anxiety is a plausible contributor (not the only one).
I hope you found this blog on Agentic Systems helpful and learned a few things! Let me know what you build :)!
References
- https://arxiv.org/abs/2512.08296

- https://cognition.ai/blog/dont-build-multi-agents
- https://cognition.ai/blog/devin-sonnet-4-5-lessons-and-challenges#the-model-is-aware-of-its-context-window
- https://karpathy.bearblog.dev/year-in-review-2025/
